findings.title: Queued follow-up after `/new` can skip context injection entirely
findings.description: `needs_context` is only consumed on the direct execution path, before the message is enqueued. If the session is already running, `/new` sets the flag and is queued raw. Any next user message that arrives before the worker finishes is also queued raw because the `!acquired` branch explicitly avoids inject/consume. When the worker later drains the queue, it sees `/new` and then the follow-up message exactly as enqueued, so the intended 'inject on the next message after `/new`' behavior is lost in a common mid-turn case.
findings.suggestion: Move the inject/consume decision to dequeue time inside the session worker, or persist an explicit per-message/per-session marker that is applied when the next message is actually sent to Claude.

findings.title: `needs_context` can remain armed even when `/new` was never delivered
findings.description: The flag is inserted before `send_message` succeeds, but it is only cleared later on a successful direct-path non-`/new` message. If queueing `/new` fails because the queue is full, the session is missing, or `send_message` returns another error, the flag survives and the next unrelated message gets prefixed with injected context even though the reset command never reached Claude. The same pattern is duplicated in `execute_in_session`.
findings.suggestion: Only arm the flag after `/new` has been successfully enqueued, or clear it on every enqueue/send failure path. Putting this in one helper would keep `handle_message` and `execute_in_session` consistent.

findings.title: Thread names are injected into a privileged prompt wrapper without escaping
findings.description: `session_context()` interpolates `thread_name` directly inside `<system-reminder>...</system-reminder>`. Discord thread names are user-controlled, so a crafted name containing closing tags or instruction text can break the wrapper and smuggle prompt content into what is supposed to be system-side context.
findings.suggestion: Escape or serialize the thread name before embedding it, or keep the thread name outside the privileged wrapper entirely.

findings.title: Global `Arc<Mutex<HashSet<_>>>` makes the inject flag hard to reason about under load
findings.description: The added `needs_context` state uses one async mutex for all threads and requires manual cleanup in message handling, delete, unregister, eviction, and TTL sweep. The new code does not hold a `MutexGuard` across `.await`, so there is no immediate async-safety bug here, but the design spreads a single logical state machine across multiple call sites and makes lock contention and missed cleanup paths more likely over time.
findings.suggestion: Store the flag with per-session state in `SessionManager` and make 'consume first post-`/new` message' part of one atomic session transition rather than a separate global map.

summary: The added `/new` context injection flow has two correctness bugs in how the flag is armed/consumed, plus a prompt-injection risk from unsanitized thread names. I did not find a new `MutexGuard`-across-`.await` issue in the added code.
